chunk_summary: This section outlines structured modules for physical activity, sleep, screen time, and behavior skills as part of intensive lifestyle treatment for managing overnutrition and obesity in outpatient settings, with a focus on adherence, relapse prevention, and age-appropriate guidelines.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of physical activity, sleep, screen time, and behavior skills in obesity management.
Learn to prescribe age-appropriate physical activity and sleep routines for children and adolescents.
Identify strategies for reducing screen time and promoting healthy household behaviors.
Develop relapse prevention plans for maintaining lifestyle changes during disruptions.
key_facts: Children aged 6–12 years require 9–12 hours of sleep per night, while adolescents aged 13–18 years need 8–10 hours.
Physical activity should average 60 minutes per day of moderate-to-vigorous activity, starting with 15-minute sessions and increasing weekly.
Strength training for adolescents is recommended 2–3 days per week, including bodyweight exercises like squats, pushups, and planks.
Screen time should be limited to no screens during meals and no screens for 60 minutes before sleep.
Relapse prevention strategies should include a 'minimum viable routine' during disruptions, such as maintaining a sleep cutoff and beverage rules.
reference_quote: "Children aged 6–12 years should regularly sleep 9–12 hours per 24 hours, and teenagers aged 13–18 years should sleep 8–10 hours per 24 hours." (American Academy of Sleep Medicine, 2016)
document_key_topics: Physical activity guidelines for children and adolescents
Sleep and screen time management in obesity treatment
Behavioral and household strategies for lifestyle adherence
Relapse prevention in outpatient obesity care
